The views over the Columbia Gorge were absolutely stunning on a sunny day in July, though I definitely recommend starting early to beat the heat. It's a popular trail, so parking wasn’t an issue, but be prepared for some rocky sections and plenty of switchbacks.  I even spotted a cheeky chipmunk along the way!

trail_name: Angel's Rest to Devil's Rest Loop
review_text: August 24th was perfect weather for this one - brilliantly sunny and thankfully dry. That view from Angel’s Rest is absolutely worth the climb, but honestly, the last push up to Devil’s Rest was pretty overgrown in places; sturdy boots are a must. It's a challenging hike and I wouldn't recommend it for beginners, but those looking for a rewarding workout with incredible panoramic views should definitely consider tackling this loop.

The elevation gain – over five thousand feet – really takes it out of you, and honestly, I’d recommend starting your hike *way* before 9am to snag a parking spot. We were lucky to get there early enough to enjoy the trail mostly dry, though the waterfalls were flowing pretty well after some recent rain. The views from the top are worth the sweat, but be prepared for a tough workout and bring plenty of water!

trail_name: Mt Defiance via Starvation Creek Trail
review_text: This climb up Mt. Defiance is seriously no joke – I’d rate it as one of the toughest hikes I've done in the Gorge this summer. The elevation gain hits you hard, especially the last few thousand feet, but the views from the top were absolutely worth the burn. It was a beautiful sunny day for it too, though you definitely need to bring plenty of water; there’s not much shade up there. I only saw a handful of other hikers, which made the experience feel pretty peaceful, and parking at the trailhead was surprisingly easy.

trail_name: Mt Defiance via Starvation Creek Trail
review_text: This was a brutal climb, no kidding. The Starvation Creek Trail to Mt. Defiance is seriously relentless – that 5177 feet of elevation gain just keeps coming at you. I went on Saturday, September 2nd, and it was absolutely pouring rain the whole way up, so traction was definitely a concern; poles are a must. Despite the miserable weather, the views overlooking the Columbia River Gorge were worth pushing through for, though honestly, I wouldn’t recommend this one unless you're prepared for a serious workout and some seriously soggy boots.

trail_name: Mt Defiance via Starvation Creek Trail
review_text: This one was brutal. Mt. Defiance lives up to its name – seriously relentless elevation gain, pushing a solid 5177 feet. The overgrowth was absolutely insane at times, requiring some serious bushwhacking and slowing us down considerably, especially around the summit ridge. It's beautiful up there though, and worth the effort if you’re prepared for a really tough day out, just be aware parking is scarce.

Honestly, you’ll want poles on this one; those granite slabs were relentless, and the views were obscured by the clouds, though the sense of accomplishment at the summit was worth every aching muscle.

The Ptarmigan Trail section was relentlessly steep – nearly 4700 feet of climbing in just over eight miles, and I’m not gonna lie, my legs were screaming by the time I hit the summit. The volcanic landscape is certainly a sight to behold, a truly dramatic backdrop to what's otherwise a demanding day out; it felt a bit exposed up there with the sunshine, so bring layers. It was pretty crowded too, which took away from the solitude, but overall, a solid accomplishment if you’re prepared for a serious climb.

trail_name: Mt. St Helens Summit via Ptarmigan Trail
review_text: This climb up Mt. St. Helens is no joke; seriously, bring plenty of water and electrolytes if you’re thinking about tackling it. The Ptarmigan Trail itself felt relentless with that 4639 feet of elevation gain – it’s a long day, pushing nearly eight miles to the summit. I was really struck by the boulder fields near the top, a strange and beautiful landscape after all that climbing. It was a bright, sunny day which helped immensely, though the overgrowth got pretty thick at times, making for some tough scrambling.

trail_name: Mt. St Helens Summit via Ptarmigan Trail
review_text: The Ptarmigan Trail up to Mt. St. Helens is no joke – seriously, this hike earns its 5/5 difficulty rating. The fog was thick that day, making visibility pretty limited and honestly, a bit unnerving at times, but the views when it briefly cleared were absolutely worth it. It’s a long haul with over 4600 feet of elevation gain, so pace yourself; I saw a couple of mountain goats grazing near the summit which was a cool sight. Parking was decent, though you'll definitely want to arrive early on a weekend.

trail_name: Mt. St Helens Summit via Ptarmigan Trail
review_text: Wow, this hike up Mount St. Helens was brutal but seriously rewarding. The Ptarmigan Trail is a beast – nearly 9 miles with over 4600 feet of elevation gain – and it’s relentless. We started pretty early, like before 8am, to snag parking, which was already packed. There was still plenty of snow higher up, even in June, making traction tricky at times, but the views from the top were absolutely incredible, a real testament to the volcano's power.

trail_name: Mt. St Helens Summit via Ptarmigan Trail
review_text: Wow, this climb up Mt. St Helens is no joke. Reaching the crater rim was absolutely rewarding, seeing that stark landscape firsthand, but honestly, be prepared for a long day; this one took us nearly eight hours round trip. Parking at the visitor center is incredibly limited, so get there early or you'll be waiting.

trail_name: Mt. St Helens Summit via Ptarmigan Trail
review_text: This was brutal. Seriously brutal. The Ptarmigan Trail to the summit of Mt. St. Helens is no joke, and I’d give it a solid five stars for challenge alone. It's mostly unrelenting switchbacks up through some seriously rocky terrain – we’re talking boulder fields that really test your legs. The views were incredible as you got closer to the top, but honestly, by the time I reached the summit, my quads were screaming. Bring plenty of water and electrolytes; you’ll need ‘em.

trail_name: Mt. St Helens Summit via Ptarmigan Trail
review_text: This climb up to the summit of Mt. St. Helens is seriously demanding – I’d rate it as one of the toughest hikes I’ve done this year. The elevation gain hits you hard, especially on the steeper sections near the top, and the trail itself is relentlessly rocky. It was a cloudy day which made navigation a little tricky at times with the volcanic landscape stretching out in every direction, but spotting a mountain goat grazing near the summit was a nice reward for the effort. Parking was incredibly limited so get there early if you want to snag a spot!

review_text: It was a brutal climb up to the summit of Mt. St. Helens today, and I mean *brutal*. The Ptarmigan Trail is no joke; that 4639 feet of elevation gain just keeps coming at you. trail_name: Mt. St Helens Summit via Ptarmigan Trail
review_text: Wow, this climb up Mt. St. Helens is no joke. The Ptarmigan Trail itself was pretty rocky in spots, and that final push to the summit – seriously, a brutal scree section! review_text: Honestly, this was one of the toughest climbs I’ve tackled in a while.
